mapping.a_element: matrix product form representing the steady-state distribution of microstates in driven diffusive systems
mapping.b_element: block-diagonalization or orbit theory to exploit symmetries in the transition probability matrix of a Markov chain

mapping.a_element: macroscopic physical quantities derived from the matrix product state (e.g., phase transitions, shocks)
mapping.b_element: optimization of the second-largest eigenvalue modulus to determine mixing time in Markov chains

mapping.a_element: algebraic structure (matrix product) enabling exact solutions for steady states
mapping.b_element: algebraic structure (symmetry exploitation) enabling efficient computation of optimal transition probabilities

brief_justification: While both papers leverage algebraic structures to simplify complex systems (matrix product states for nonequilibrium steady states vs. symmetry exploitation for Markov chains), the underlying machinery differs: Paper A focuses on exact solutions via matrix product ansatz for steady-state distributions, whereas Paper B optimizes spectral properties of transition matrices using symmetry reductions. The core mechanisms (matrix product vs. symmetry-based eigenvalue optimization) are distinct.
